For the 4-bit version (38.7 t/s, ~21 GB, better quality than 3-bit), here are targeted suggestions to squeeze more performance or improve the quality/speed balance while keeping the higher fidelity. 1. Quick Parameter & Sampler Tweaks (Biggest Easy Wins) The default sampler is good, but you can push decode TPS closer to 42+ without much quality loss: Pythonfrom mlx_lm import load, generate from mlx_lm.sample_utils import make_sampler model, tokenizer = load("unsloth/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-UD-MLX-4bit") sampler = make_sampler( temp=0.7, # or 0.6-0.65 for more focused/coding outputs top_p=0.92, # Slightly tighter than 0.95 → faster + coherent top_k=30, # Lower from 40 reduces branching min_p=0.05, # Raise from 0.1 for less "junk" tokens repetition_penalty=1.05 # Light penalty helps speed on long gens ) response = generate( model=model, tokenizer=tokenizer, prompt=your_prompt, max_tokens=16384, sampler=sampler, verbose=True, # New/important: repetition_context_size=512, # Helps long-context speed # Try this for speculative-like boost if available in your mlx-lm version: # draft_model="unsloth/Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct-MLX-4bit" # test carefully on MoE ) Test these combos (run 5-10 short benchmarks): temp=0.65 + top_p=0.90 + min_p=0.05 → often +3-6 t/s with minimal coherence drop. For coding/agentic work: lower temp (0.6) + stronger repetition_penalty. 2. Server & Prefill Optimizations Update your start-mlx-server.sh with these flags for better real-world throughput: Bashmlx_lm.server \ --model unsloth/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-UD-MLX-4bit \ --port 8001 \ --prefill-step-size 4096 \ # or 8192 — balances TTFT vs memory --max-kv-size 131072 \ # Explicit 128K --batch-size 1 \ # Single user, but try 2 if multi --trust-remote-code Higher prefill-step-size (8192+) helps long-context prompt processing speed (your research noted it doesn't affect decode much, but improves overall feel). Add --quantize or check for any 4-bit specific cache tweaks in latest mlx-lm. 3. Quality-Focused Tradeoffs (Why Use 4-bit Over 3-bit) Better for: Complex reasoning, tool calling, vision (if using mlx-vlm), long multi-turn agents, creative tasks. 3-bit is great for raw speed but can lose nuance on edge cases. Memory headroom: At 21 GB you still have ~15 GB free on 36 GB — plenty for 128K KV cache + system. Hybrid setup: Use 4-bit as primary, fall back to 3-bit for very long / high-volume sessions via your models.json routing. 4. Advanced / Experimental Speculative decoding (if your mlx-lm version supports a draft model): A small 1-2B Qwen MLX draft can add 20-40% on some workloads, but test it — MoE models sometimes see neutral or negative gains due to expert routing overhead. Dtype / precision: Force fp16 if the 4-bit load defaults to bf16 (small win on M3). Update everything: pip install -U mlx mlx-lm — monthly gains are common for Qwen MoE. Benchmark script tweak: Add torch.compile style options or custom kernels if available in Unsloth's MLX fork.

also see suggestions from grok: **Got it — that's exactly the problem.** The warning **"vocab not compatible — tokens will be translated"** + the crash to **37 tok/s** is 100% caused by the **Qwen3-0.6B** draft using an older tokenizer (vocab size ~151k) while your **Qwen3.6-35B-A3B** uses the newer Qwen3.5/3.6 tokenizer (vocab size **248,320**). llama.cpp has to translate every drafted token on the fly → massive overhead that completely kills speculative decoding speed (and often makes it slower than baseline). ### Best Draft Model for Your Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (Exact Vocab Match) Use **unsloth/Qwen3.5-0.8B-GGUF** (any quant). - It has the **exact same 248,320 vocab** as your Qwen3.6-35B-A3B. - This is the officially recommended draft in every Qwen3.6 benchmark right now. - Tiny, fast, and fits easily alongside the 35B on a single 5090. **Recommended files to download** (pick one): - `Qwen3.5-0.8B-UD-Q4_K_M.gguf` ← sweet spot (fast + good quality) - `Qwen3.5-0.8B-UD-Q3_K_M.gguf` ← even lighter if you want max speed - Repo: https://huggingface.co/unsloth/Qwen3.5-0.8B-GGUF ### How to Add It in Your Vast.ai Llama.cpp Template 1. SSH into the instance (or use the console). 2. Download the draft (example): ```bash mkdir -p /workspace/models/draft wget -O /workspace/models/draft/Qwen3.5-0.8B-Q4_K_M.gguf \ https://huggingface.co/unsloth/Qwen3.5-0.8B-GGUF/resolve/main/Qwen3.5-0.8B-UD-Q4_K_M.gguf ``` 3. Update your `LLAMA_ARGS` (add the draft flag): ```bash --model-draft /workspace/models/draft/Qwen3.5-0.8B-Q4_K_M.gguf \ --speculative-ngram 0 \ # disable ngram if you want pure classic draft ``` (Or just append it to your existing `LLAMA_ARGS` env var and restart the server.) Once loaded you should see **no more "vocab not compatible"** warning and draft acceptance rate >70–90% on most prompts. ### Expected Speed After Fix (1× RTX 5090) - With **matching 0.8B draft** → **140–210+ t/s** is realistic again (the translation penalty was the killer). - Some Qwen3.6 MoE users on 5090/4090 are seeing 1.6–2.2× over baseline with classic draft. - If it still feels slow → try the **ngram** alternative below (no extra model needed). ### Even Easier Alternative: n-gram Speculative (No Draft Model) Many people on Qwen3.6 are ditching the classic draft entirely and just using: ```bash --spec-type ngram-mod \ --spec-ngram-size-n 24 \ --spec-ngram-size-m 12 ``` → Zero extra VRAM, no vocab issues, and often **faster overall** on MoE models in llama.cpp right now. what about llama cpp MTP **Deep dive on llama.cpp MTP (Multi-Token Prediction) support for your local agent on M3 36 GB** MTP is **built-in speculative decoding** using the model’s own extra prediction heads (no separate draft model needed). Qwen3.6 family was trained with these heads (“MTP: trained with multi-steps”), so the model can draft 2–4 tokens ahead in one forward pass, verify them, and accept batches when they match. This is mathematically correct sampling (not greedy-only), and acceptance rates are typically high (~75 % on code/agentic tasks). ### Current Status (as of May 5, 2026) - **Beta feature** (merged ~May 4 via PR #22673). - **Explicitly tested** on **Qwen3.6-27B** and **Qwen3.6-35B-A3B** (your exact model). - Works by loading the MTP layers from the **same GGUF** (they are kept as extra decoder blocks). - Gains reported: **1.8–2.2×** decode speedup on compatible hardware (e.g., 23 → 42 t/s on RTX 3090 with n=3). - Your MoE hybrid (10 full-attention + 30 GatedDeltaNet layers) is supported, but the recurrent state in GatedDeltaNet makes rollback slightly more expensive than on pure dense models. ### How to Try It on Your M3 36 GB Right Now 1. **Rebuild llama.cpp to latest master** (MTP is brand new): ```bash cd ~/llama.cpp git pull make clean make -j $(sysctl -n hw.logicalcpu) LLAMA_METAL=1 ``` 2. **Use a dedicated MTP-preserving GGUF** (Unsloth regular quants strip or partially support MTP heads — use these instead): - For your model → `am17an/Qwen3.6-35BA3B-MTP-GGUF` (Q4_K_M or Q3_K_M recommended). - Or the 27B variant if you want to test lighter first. 3. **Server command example** (add to your existing start script): ```bash ./llama-server \ --model /path/to/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-MTP-Q4_K_M.gguf \ --spec-type mtp \ # ← this is the key flag --spec-draft-n-max 3 \ # 2–4 is sweet spot (higher = more speedup but lower acceptance) --host 127.0.0.1 \ --port 8001 \ -c 131072 \ -n 16384 \ -fa on \ -ctk q8_0 -ctv q8_0 \ --parallel 1 \ --no-context-shift \ --temp 0.7 --top-p 0.95 --min-p 0.1 \ --chat-template-kwargs '{"preserve_thinking": true}' ``` **Expected on your M3 36 GB**: - Baseline (your current MLX 3-bit or llama.cpp): 36–52 t/s. - With MTP (n=3): **~55–80+ t/s** on coding/agentic prompts (if it runs stably). - Memory overhead: +~2–3 GB (extra KV for MTP head). ### Important Caveats for Your Setup - **Metal backend on M3**: Still **beta / experimental**. The PR notes that Metal (and Vulkan) may fail or produce garbage output because of missing `keep_intermediates=true` support for GatedDeltaNet layers. Some users see crashes after 50–200 tokens or silent failures. - MoE + hybrid attention makes rollback slightly heavier than on pure dense Qwen3.6-27B. - Prefill phase slows down a bit (normal for any speculative method). - If it crashes or gives nonsense → fall back to your proven `--spec-type ngram-mod` (which you already got +19 % on code) or small Qwen3-1.7B draft. ### How This Helps Your Local Agent Specifically - **Coding / agentic workflows** → Highest benefit (MTP shines on predictable, structured output). - **Tool calling & multi-step reasoning** → Faster iteration without quality loss. - **Long sessions** → Combined with your TurboQuant KV cache, it keeps the agent feeling snappy even at 128K context. - No extra draft model loading = simpler than your previous 0.6B draft experiments. ### Recommendation Right Now 1. **Test it today** (low risk): Rebuild + try the MTP GGUF with `--spec-draft-n-max 2` first on short coding prompts. Watch for crashes / garbage. 2. If Metal is unstable → stick with your current **ngram-mod** + TurboQuant stack (already very strong and stable). 3. Watch the PR for Metal fixes (they usually land within days/weeks on Apple Silicon). This is one of the biggest “free” speedups available for your exact model family right now — no new hardware or cloud needed. # 1.4.0 Roadmap: Slack Contextual Actions

## Summary

Create `hub-ack-slack.md` - the missing contextual action handler for Slack VIP DM alerts. Users can view messages and send smart replies directly from Claude Code.

## Scope

**In scope:**
- `hub-ack-slack.md` skill with view + smart reply
- Chrome MCP as primary connection method
- Template-based replies with AI context adaptation

**Out of scope (future):**
- Knowledge source search (Confluence/Notion)
- Focus mode Slack status integration
- Playwright fallback

## Implementation

### 1. Create `hub-ack-slack.md`

**Location:** `plugins/claude-status-hub/skills/hub-ack-slack.md`

**Flow:**
```
Alert detected → /hub-ack → Route to hub-ack-slack.md →
  1. Fetch message details via Chrome MCP
  2. Display: sender, timestamp, message preview
  3. Present reply options (templates + AI-adapted)
  4. User selects → Send via Chrome MCP
  5. Mark alert as handled in bridge
```

**Reply templates:**
- "On it!"
- "Let me check and get back to you"
- "Thanks, will follow up"
- "Got it, working on this now"
- AI adapts template based on message context (question vs request vs FYI)

### 2. Update `hub-ack.md` routing

Add Slack alert type detection and routing to `hub-ack-slack.md`.

### 3. Chrome MCP integration for replies

Use existing `tool-slack.md` patterns:
- Find Slack tab
- Navigate to DM conversation
- Input reply text
- Send message

## Files to modify

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `skills/hub-ack-slack.md` | **CREATE** - Main skill |
| `skills/hub-ack.md` | Add Slack routing |
| `skills/tool-slack.md` | Add reply/send functions if needed |
| `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` | Bump to 1.4.0 |
| `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` | Bump to 1.4.0 |

1. Setup: Have Slack tab open with VIP DM configured
2. Trigger: Receive DM from VIP person
3. Refresh: `/hub` should show Slack alert
4. Ack: `/hub-ack` should route to Slack handler
5. View: Should display message preview
6. Reply: Select template → message sent via Chrome MCP
7. Clear: Alert marked as handled
